questions.title: Explain the CSS box model.
questions.shortAnswer: Every element is a box made of content, padding, border, and margin. box-sizing controls whether width/height includes padding+border.
questions.detailedExplanation: By default (content-box), width sets only the content area; padding and border add to total size. border-box includes padding and border in the declared width, which makes layout math predictable.
questions.realWorldExample: Resetting * { box-sizing: border-box } is standard in modern CSS so a 300px card stays 300px after padding.
questions.interviewTip: Mention margin collapsing — a classic follow-up.

questions.title: Flexbox vs Grid — when do you use each?
questions.shortAnswer: Flexbox is one-dimensional (row or column). Grid is two-dimensional (rows and columns). Use Grid for page layouts, Flex for component alignment.
questions.detailedExplanation: Grid gives explicit track control and named areas, ideal for app shells. Flex is unbeatable for toolbars, nav, and content that needs to wrap on a single axis.
questions.realWorldExample: A dashboard uses Grid for the sidebar+main split and Flex inside the header for logo, search, and avatar.
bestPractices: Combine them — Grid outside, Flex inside

codeSnippet.code:
.app { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 240px 1fr; }
.header { display: flex; gap: 1rem; align-items: center; }

questions.interviewTip: Say 'Grid for layout, Flex for components' — it's the canonical answer.

questions.title: What is CSS specificity?
questions.shortAnswer: A weight system (inline > id > class/attr/pseudo-class > element) that decides which rule wins when multiple selectors target the same element.
questions.detailedExplanation: Specificity is calculated per selector (a,b,c,d). Higher tuple wins; ties go to source order. !important overrides but should be avoided.
questions.realWorldExample: Tailwind utility classes work because they're single class selectors (0,1,0) — predictable to override.
commonMistakes: Specificity wars with deeply nested selectors
questions.interviewTip: Mention @layer — it lets you sidestep specificity battles in modern CSS.

questions.title: What are pseudo-classes vs pseudo-elements?
questions.shortAnswer: Pseudo-classes (:hover, :focus) target element state. Pseudo-elements (::before, ::after) create or target sub-parts of an element.
questions.detailedExplanation: Pseudo-classes use single colon, pseudo-elements use double colon (modern syntax). ::before / ::after require content property to render.
questions.realWorldExample: Tooltip arrows are commonly built with ::after positioned absolutely outside the element.
commonMistakes: Using single colon for pseudo-elements (works but legacy)

codeSnippet.code: .tooltip::after { content: ''; position: absolute; border-top: 6px solid black; }

questions.interviewTip: Bring up :has() — modern parent selector is a hot topic.

questions.title: How do CSS variables (custom properties) work?
questions.shortAnswer: --name declares a value scoped to its element; var(--name) reads it. They cascade and can be overridden per scope — perfect for theming.
questions.detailedExplanation: Unlike Sass variables, they live at runtime, can be changed via JS, and react to media queries — enabling dark mode without re-bundling CSS.
questions.realWorldExample: Set --bg on :root for light mode and override on .dark for dark mode. Toggle a class on <html> to switch themes.
questions.interviewTip: Mention @property — it lets you type custom props for animation.
